chunks.text: \chapter{EECS Instructional Computer Facilities } {\it The information in this section, with any current updates, is also available on-line in the file /usr/pub/EECS.facilities. See Section \ref{info}.} \section{Overview of EECS Facilities} \inde

o}.} \section{Overview of EECS Facilities} \index{EECS Facilities} Technical and Computing Services (TCS) maintains a variety of computer systems for use by EECS students, professors, researchers and staff. All of the systems are on the Ethernet

and staff. All of the systems are on the Ethernet (provides local networking) and most are linked to the Internet (provides world-wide networking). These systems all run the UNIX\/\TM operating system. There are also 486-based ATs running MS-DOS.

tem. There are also 486-based ATs running MS-DOS. TCS operates independently of the Central Computing Services (CCS) but buys time on CCS facilities for Instructional use. CCS user assistance and first-line support is available at the CCS Helpdes

first-line support is available at the CCS Helpdesk (WEB Office, Evans Hall basement, helpdesk@web, 643-9661). Regular CCS consulting is done at the CCS Consulting Office, 206 Evans Hall, 642-4072. Accounts on CCS-supported systems (CMSA, garnet, v

Accounts on CCS-supported systems (CMSA, garnet, violet, uclink) are given out at 241 Evans, 642-7355. Here are some systems commonly used for EECS instruction: \label{EECS Facilities} \index{EECS Facilities} \par \begin{tabular}{l l} Laboratory &

acilities} \par \begin{tabular}{l l} Laboratory & Equipment \\ \hline \\ 105 Cory & HP 715/33 workstations\\ 111 Cory & Macintoshes and PCs for special classes (pending) \\ 119 Cory & DEC 2100 workstations \\ 123 Cory & Intel 486 PCs and print

ations \\ 123 Cory & Intel 486 PCs and printers \\ 199 Cory & HP 715/33 and DEC 5000/25 workstations\\ 273 Soda & HP 712/60 workstations\\ 274 Soda & `lw274' laser printers \\ 275 Soda & HP 712/60 workstations\\ 347 Soda & HP 71 5/80 wor

P 712/60 workstations\\ 347 Soda & HP 71 5/80 workstations\\ 349 Soda & HP 715/80 and SGI workstations\\ \end{tabular} Additionally, there is usually a laser printer in each lab. \section{Individual Accounts for EECS Faculty} \index{accounts!fac

ual Accounts for EECS Faculty} \index{accounts!faculty} EECS faculty members may request a personal account by sending e-mail to ``root@hera''. \section{Computer Accounts for Students} \index{accounts!computer} \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em What is

ts!computer} \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em What is an ``instructional'' account?} The Department of Electrical Engineering \& Computer Sciences maintains its own computers to be used for EECS coursework. Most EECS students will be issued {\it named}

ork. Most EECS students will be issued {\it named} accounts only instead of {\it class} accounts. A named account has a login that resembles the user's name; a class account login resembles the course name (such as ``ee141-xx''). There is no fee req

name (such as ``ee141-xx''). There is no fee required for any account on the Instructional computers. The {\it named} accounts will be given access to all of the Instructional computers in Cory and Soda Halls, including the HP, DEC and Silicon Grap

Soda Halls, including the HP, DEC and Silicon Graphics workstations and the multi-user machines such as Cory and Po. For most courses, no other accounts will be issued --- students will use their named accounts for all coursework. The password and ho

d accounts for all coursework. The password and home directory space will be the same when accessed from any Instructional UNIX computer. A few classes will still be issued {\it class} accounts. Class accounts are created at the request of faculty,

s accounts are created at the request of faculty, who distribute account forms to the students in class. Students do not need to request {\it class} accounts. Class accounts expire at the end of the semester. \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em Who is qua

semester. \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em Who is qualified to have an instructional account?} Undergraduates who are declared as EECS or L\&S CS majors, EE and CS graduate students and CS Reentry students can request long-term {\it named} accounts on

nts can request long-term {\it named} accounts on the EECS Instructional systems. The account will remain active as long as the student is in the department. It will not expire or be turned off at the end of each semester. For undeclared and non-EEC

end of each semester. For undeclared and non-EECS majors, a single named account will usually be issued for all classes being taken. This account will expire at the end of the semester but will be restored if the user enrolls in another EECS course

estored if the user enrolls in another EECS course the following semester. Undeclared students in CS Lower Division courses who are candidates for acceptance in the major may be eligible for permanent {\it named} accounts, with the approval of the CS

{\it named} accounts, with the approval of the CS Division office (Barbara Hightower, 339 Soda Hall). \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em What computers can I use for EECS coursework?} The EECS Instructional systems are UNIX and MS-DOS systems. They are

nal systems are UNIX and MS-DOS systems. They are located in the southwest corner of the first floor of Cory Hall and the second and third floors of Soda Hall. The Instructional computers can be reached via modem numbers (such as 642-6679). For a cu

hed via modem numbers (such as 642-6679). For a current description of the facilities, please read the files {\bf /usr/pub/EECS.facilities} and {\bf /usr/pub/dialups} on any EECS Instructional system. Current information is also available using ``gop

Current information is also available using ``gopher'' and is posted on bulletin boards in the EECS computer labs. Although EECS students will have access to a variety of computers, many instructors will specify a particular architecture (DEC, HP,

will specify a particular architecture (DEC, HP, Silicon Graphics, PC) where the software for that course runs best. The file {\bf /usr/pub/Tbl\_of\_Classes} includes information about software and computers provided for specific classes. Some clas

omputers provided for specific classes. Some classes will be assigned semesterly ``class'' accounts on non-EECS systems, such as the Webworm Sun Sparc cluster. The EECS instructional ``named'' accounts will not be valid on those machines nor on sys

nts will not be valid on those machines nor on systems such as uclink. \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em How do I obtain my instructional account?} {\bf New accounts:} \\ To request a new ``named'' account, users should login to any EECS Instructional s

nt, users should login to any EECS Instructional system as the user ``newacct''. You can do this in 2 ways: 1) Type ``{\bf newacct}'' at the login window of any workstation in an EECS lab, such as 105 Cory or 273 Soda. 2) Type ``{\bf rlo

uch as 105 Cory or 273 Soda. 2) Type ``{\bf rlogin cory.eecs -l newacct}'' from another UNIX computer account or over a modem if you call from home. You will be asked for your Student ID number and name. If your entries are valid, you will

ber and name. If your entries are valid, you will be asked to choose a login (i.e. your ``computer name''). The account will usually be created by 1PM on the next business day, and you can go to 391 Cory after that to obtain your account form and pas

ory after that to obtain your account form and password. You will have to show a valid student ID card at that time. The {\bf newacct} procedure may reject the requests for some users, typically if they do not have a Student ID Number or if they are

hey do not have a Student ID Number or if they are not pre-enrolled in any EECS course. In that case, {\bf newacct} will inform the student to complete an EECS Instructional Account Request Form, available outside 386 Cory. When you first log in to

ilable outside 386 Cory. When you first log in to your new account, you'll be asked to enter the EECS classes you are taking. Later, you can use the ``validate'' command to modify that. This information may be used to allow you to access software th

ion may be used to allow you to access software that is restricted to your class. \noindent $\Rightarrow$ {\em What do I go for help about an instructional account?} If you have trouble with your account, contact a member of the EECS Instructional

count, contact a member of the EECS Instructional group by sending email to {\bf root@cory.eecs} (NOT root@eecs or root@cs) or visiting {\bf 384/386 Cory Hall or 333 Soda Hall.} Tutorial help and guidance about UNIX, the terminals and printers is a

idance about UNIX, the terminals and printers is also available from the {\bf XCF} group of student volunteers in 311 Soda or from the CSUA volunteers in 343 Soda. \section{Other Accounts for Students} \index{accounts!student} The following comput

ts} \index{accounts!student} The following computer facilities are available for students to get personal, long-term computer accounts. All are on the campus network and can be reached by modems. \begin{table}[hbt] \begin{tabular}{l l l} System &

begin{table}[hbt] \begin{tabular}{l l l} System & Location & Account eligibility, cost, and \\ name & of terminals & where to ask questions \\ \hline \\ soda & 343 Soda & members of the CSUA; free; go to 343 Soda Hall\\ & & or send e-mail to ``root@

to 343 Soda Hall\\ & & or send e-mail to ``root@soda''. \\ ocf & 72 Barrows & all UCB students; free; go to 72 Barrows Hall\\ & & or send e-mail to ``root@ocf''. \\ uclink & various & UCB students, staff and faculty; free; go to 241 Evans,\\ & &

, staff and faculty; free; go to 241 Evans,\\ & & send e-mail to ``consult@uclink'' or call 642-7355. \end{tabular} \end{table} \section{Services Provided to EECS Faculty} \subsection{Technical Support} Instructors and TAs who detect a problem wit

ort} Instructors and TAs who detect a problem with the Instructional facilities are requested to call or send e-mail to the Manager of Instructional Systems (Kevin Mullally, 643-6141, kevinm@cory) or to call the main TCS number to leave a message (

r to call the main TCS number to leave a message (642-6744). \subsection{Application Software Support} \index{software} The Instructional Systems Support Group has the task of providing computer systems and peripherals, and the standard UNIX opera

stems and peripherals, and the standard UNIX operating system software for EECS instructional lab work. Other popular application programs are supported, such as Chez Scheme, Saber C, Franz Lisp, and SUPREM. (Run the {\bf software} command on most E

SUPREM. (Run the {\bf software} command on most EECS Instructional systems to see more information about supported software.) \index{software@{{\bf software} command}} Other applications used for instruction are maintained by the instructors. When

nstruction are maintained by the instructors. When an instructor wishes to install a new application, he or she should consult with the Manager of Instructional Systems. Issues to be considered will include the system load, costs and maintenance effo

nclude the system load, costs and maintenance efforts that the software will impose. For vendor-supplied software, our staff usually will do the installation and keep the tapes and documentation. The staff may provide a copy of the documentation fo

e staff may provide a copy of the documentation for use in the lab room, but the instructor is responsible for making copies for the entire class. If the instructor provides the software, our staff will install it if ``root'' permission is required.

ill install it if ``root'' permission is required. The tasks of debugging and testing are the responsibility of the user. \section{What Faculty Should Do for Help} \index{problems!help for faculty} \index{help|see{problems}} \index{errors|see{prob

\index{help|see{problems}} \index{errors|see{problems}} Faculty and TAs are encouraged to contact the Administrator of the system they are using, either by e-mail or by phone. This will assure quick attention to the problem. For Instructional com

ck attention to the problem. For Instructional computer systems, contact the Manager of Instructional Systems: Kevin Mullally, 643-6141, kevinm@cory. Also, all of the methods described in the following section are available. \section{What Students

ing section are available. \section{What Students Should Do for Help}\label{info} \index{problems!help for students} There are many different ways to find information or obtain help. Depending on the nature of your question or problem, you may want

e nature of your question or problem, you may want to use one or more of the following methods for obtaining assistance: \begin{enumerate} \item{Read the on-line Manual Pages. (See Section \ref{man:command}.)} \item{Read the various help files sto

:command}.)} \item{Read the various help files stored in the /usr/pub directory of all EECS Instructional computers. Type ``{\bf cd /usr/pub; ls}'' for a list of files.} \item{Use the ``gopher'' program. (See Section \ref{gopher}.)} \item{Read the v

am. (See Section \ref{gopher}.)} \item{Read the various help files available via ``anonymous FTP''. (See Section \ref{anonFTP}.)} \item{Read current information posted on big green bulletin boards near 105 Cory and 273 Soda and in the EECS Instruct

ear 105 Cory and 273 Soda and in the EECS Instructional lab rooms.} \item{Solicit advice and help from TCS by sending e-mail to ``root'' on your computer.} \item{See the EECS Instructional staff in 384 Cory or 333 Soda.} \item{The Experimental Comput

4 Cory or 333 Soda.} \item{The Experimental Computing Facility (XCF) offers walk-in help, tutorial classes and handouts. The XCF is run by students and is located in 311 Soda Hall.} \item{The Computer Science Undergraduate Association (CSUA) in 343

er Science Undergraduate Association (CSUA) in 343 Soda Hall can provide some assistance.} \item{Read the various help files available through ``Infocal'' by typing: \\ {\tt telnet infocal.berkeley.edu}.} \item{Use the {\bf netscape} or {\bf mosaic

edu}.} \item{Use the {\bf netscape} or {\bf mosaic} programs. See Section \ref{netscape}.)} \end{enumerate} \section{Access to Terminal Rooms and Printers} \index{card keys} \index{laboratories} Most of the labs will require access by cardkey (so

ost of the labs will require access by cardkey (so will the building itself on evenings and weekends). The Instructional labs are provided for students to complete coursework, so access is linked to the current enrollment in an EECS course. Student

the current enrollment in an EECS course. Students are pre-approved for cardkeys when their instructors submit a class list to 391 Cory. \subsection{How to get a Card Key} \begin{enumerate} \item Students are given access permission by their instr

tudents are given access permission by their instructors, who submit a signed class list to 391 Cory. \item Once the list has been submitted, students should go to 391 Cory to obtain new cardkeys or to have an existing one updated. \end{enum

keys or to have an existing one updated. \end{enumerate} \subsection{Access Policy} \begin{itemize} \item The Instructional UNIX labs in Cory and Soda Halls are usually unlocked from 7am-6:30pm on weekdays when classes are in session. \item The

n weekdays when classes are in session. \item The PC and electronic labs will always require a cardkey. \item Access to the buildings and to individual labs will require a cardkey after 6:30pm and on weekends, vacations and holidiays. \end{itemize

on weekends, vacations and holidiays. \end{itemize} Please note that there is a campus escort service that will walk you from the labs to the dormitories or parking lots. Call 642-WALK \index{642-WALK} for assistance. This is highly recommended if

LK} for assistance. This is highly recommended if you are working in the computer labs at night. Information on card key procedures and access is subject to change. Current information about the Instructional labs is available on-line in the file /u

ructional labs is available on-line in the file /usr/pub/EECS.facilities. See Section \ref{info} for details. \section{Dialup Access} \index{dialup access} \index{modems} Users can access EECS Instructional systems via modems on the following

ic{mpfootnote}} Numbers\footnote{These numbers access a ``hunt group'' of modems. If one is busy, the next is tried automatically. Each of these hunt groups includes at least 16 modems. So, you can dial a single number to reach the next available

dial a single number to reach the next available modem in the group. \par} & Baud Rate & Device Accessed \\ \hline \\ 642-6679 & auto, up to 14.4kb & modems on a terminal server \\ 642-0070 & auto, up to 28.8kb & modems on a terminal server

42-4920. \par} \\ 643-0165 & same, with 15 minute limit & IST modems on a terminal server \(^{3}\) \\ \\ \\ \end{tabular} } \end{minipage} \par \par \par \par \par \par When connecting from a modem, use these line settings: \begin{quote} par

, use these line settings: \begin{quote} parity: none, Data bits: 8, Stop bits: 1 \\ or \\ parity: even, Data bits: 7, Stop bits: 1 \\ \end{quote} Most software will work with either setting. The first one is preferable; however, som

setting. The first one is preferable; however, some older file transfer software may only work with the second one. \subsection{Home Internet Access with PPP or SLIP}\index{slip}\index{ppp} \index{home-ip} Information Services and Technology (IST) p

me-ip} Information Services and Technology (IST) provides this service for UCB students, faculty, and staff. Please read {\bf /usr/pub/home-ip.help} for details. \subsection{Terminal Server Example} \label{annex} {\it Changes are being made to the

\label{annex} {\it Changes are being made to the campus and departmental modems during Summer 1996. In order to use the modems, you may need to provide your home-ip username and password. Please read {\bf /usr/pub/dialups} and/or {\bf /usr/pub/ho

ad {\bf /usr/pub/dialups} and/or {\bf /usr/pub/home-ip.help} for the latest details on this.} Here is an example of the screen that should appear on your terminal once the terminal server answers your modem. You may need to enter {\tt } a number o

ur modem. You may need to enter {\tt } a number of times to get the ``annex:'' prompt: {\tt \begin{verse} Annex Command Line Interpreter * Copyright 1988 Xylogics, Inc. Checking authorization, Please wait\ldots \\ >>> Welcome to Cory Ha

on, Please wait\ldots \\ >>> Welcome to Cory Hall Modems \begin{tabbing} Type ``rlogin \= -l '' to login. \\ \> (this is the letter ``el'', not the number ``one'')\\ After logging out, please type ``hangup'' to reset session.

ging out, please type ``hangup'' to reset session. \\ Type ``help'' if you need more information. \end{tabbing} annex: rlogin volga -l e243-xr \\ Password: \ldots message of the day, user's login scripts, etc \ldots \ldots

ay, user's login scripts, etc \ldots \ldots after we're done \ldots volga> logout \\ CLI: Connection closed. \\ annex: hangup Resetting line and disconnecting. \end{verse} } This information is also available on-line. See Se

This information is also available on-line. See Section \ref{info} for details. \subsection{File Transfers} The file transfer programs {\bf xmodem} and {\bf kermit} are usually available on the Instructional systems. These are common directory l

tructional systems. These are common directory locations (depending upon the system and version of the programs): \begin{quote} /usr/local \\ /usr/local/bin \\ /usr/public \\ /usr/sww/bin \\ /share/a/bin \\ \end{quote} Each has built-in help infor

a/bin \\ \end{quote} Each has built-in help information which is displayed if you type {\it kermit} or {\it xmodem}. The ZMODEM protocol is supported by the programs {\bf sz} and {\bf rz}, usually located in the one of the above directories. For m
ocated in the one of the above directories. For more detailed information about file transfers, please see the file /usr/pub/dialups or the information on Gopher.
